title: İmam bayıldı
subtitle: Turkish braised aubergine stuffed with onion, garlic, and tomato.
excerpt: İmam bayıldı is the Turkish olive oil dish of braised aubergine: whole aubergines split and packed with softened onion, garlic, and tomato, then submerged in olive oil and cooked slowly until the flesh collapses. It is served at room temperature and is better the day after it is made.
sourceNotes: A classic of the zeytinyağlı tradition of Turkish olive oil cookery, in which vegetables are braised in a generous quantity of olive oil and served cold or at room temperature. The dish is documented across Ottoman and modern Turkish culinary literature. The name is commonly translated as 'the imam fainted', attributed variously to the quantity of olive oil used or the pleasure of the dish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature before serving — the flavour improves overnight as the aubergine absorbs more of the oil.

recipeTools.notes: Wide, heavy-based pan with a lid. The aubergines need to sit in a single layer.

content.text: İmam bayıldı belongs to the zeytinyağlı tradition of Turkish cooking, a category of dishes made with olive oil and served cold or at room temperature. The oil is not a background note here: the aubergines spend fifty minutes braising in it, absorbing it slowly until the flesh becomes dense, silky, and sweet. The quantity of oil looks alarming before cooking. Afterwards, it looks correct.

The filling is simply softened onion, garlic, and tomato — the same combination that appears as a base across the eastern Mediterranean. It is cooked separately until soft, then packed into the split aubergines before braising. The aubergines are scored and pressed open to accept the filling; they close partially around it as they cook.

This dish is better the day after it is made. The aubergines continue to absorb the oil as they cool, and the flavour deepens overnight in the fridge. Bring them to room temperature before serving with bread to soak up the oil in the dish.

Make a deep lengthways slit in each of the {{aubergine}} aubergines, cutting from just below the stem to just above the base, leaving both ends intact. Rub the cut surfaces with a generous pinch of salt and set aside for ten minutes. Pat dry with kitchen paper.

Heat three tablespoons of the olive oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} sliced onion and cook for fifteen minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and golden. Add the {{garlic}} sliced garlic and cook for two minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, season with salt, and cook over medium-high heat for eight minutes, stirring, until the mixture is thick and jammy. Taste and adjust salt.

Open the slit in each aubergine gently and press in a generous spoonful of filling. Arrange the stuffed aubergines in the pan in a single layer. Pour the remaining olive oil over and around them, then add 100 ml of water. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a lid, and cook over low heat for forty minutes until the aubergines are completely soft and collapsing. Check occasionally and add a splash of water if the pan looks dry.

Remove from the heat and leave to cool in the pan for at least thirty minutes. Transfer to a serving dish with all the oil and juices. Squeeze the lemon juice over, scatter the parsley on top, and serve at room temperature with bread.

items.symptom: The aubergines are still firm after forty minutes
items.cause: The heat was too low, or the lid was not on properly.
items.fix: Continue cooking covered for a further ten to fifteen minutes. The aubergines should be completely soft and yielding when pressed.

items.symptom: The oil and filling have separated and look greasy
items.cause: Normal — the oil will pool around the aubergines. Tilt the pan and spoon the oil back over them as they cool. The dish settles as it rests.
Tomatoes were not reduced enough before filling.
items.fix: Simmer the filling for a further five minutes over medium-high heat before filling the aubergines, stirring until it thickens.

content.text: İmam bayıldı sits within the zeytinyağlı category of Turkish cooking: dishes made with olive oil and vegetables, cooked slowly, and served cold or at room temperature without meat. The category reflects the Ottoman influence of Byzantine fasting traditions — many of these dishes are naturally vegan. They appear on the meze table, as a first course, or as a light main. The same olive oil braising technique applies across the category to courgettes, leeks, and green beans, each following the same pattern of long cooking and room-temperature serving.
